authoritative_sources.title: Selling on the Internet: Prompt Delivery Rules
authoritative_sources.source_class: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC is the primary U.S. consumer-protection authority for unfair or deceptive commerce practices. This guidance is authoritative for shipment promises, delay notices, cancellations, and prompt refunds when merchants miss order commitments.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: order, pickup, delivery, and refund baseline review
used_for: order-commitment caveats for BOPIS and delivery analytics

authoritative_sources.title: Businessperson's Guide to Federal Warranty Law
authoritative_sources.source_class: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC's warranty business guide is the authoritative public federal baseline for written warranty disclosures, service-contract treatment, and the line between documented warranty handling and improvised remedy claims.
used_for: boundary between analysis and warranty outcomes

authoritative_sources.title: Compare Prices
authoritative_sources.source_class: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC consumer guidance is an official public reference for truthful comparison-pricing claims and consumer expectations about advertised, sale, and regular prices.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: promotional pricing and customer-facing claim review

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Handbook 130 - Current Edition
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST Office of Weights and Measures publishes the public baseline used by many U.S. jurisdictions for retail price verification and price-accuracy procedures, making it authoritative for shelf-versus-POS pricing evidence handling.
used_for: shelf, ad, and POS conflict handling

authoritative_sources.title: Electronic and Appliance Repair Dealer FAQs
authoritative_sources.publisher: California Department of Consumer Affairs, Bureau of Household Goods and Services
authoritative_sources.source_class: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: California's repair-dealer regulator publishes official guidance on written estimates, diagnosis fees, service-call charges, invoices, and additional authorizations, supplying concrete service-ticket and fee artifact expectations for a major U.S. electronics/appliance service jurisdiction.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: service-ticket, fee, and authorization artifact review
used_for: boundary between descriptive analytics and service-charge decisions

authoritative_sources.title: Recall Checklist
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CPSC is the primary U.S. regulator for consumer-product safety. Its recall checklist is authoritative workflow guidance for identifying recalled products, stopping sale, isolating inventory, and coordinating customer-record usage in recall handling.
used_for: exclusion or caveating of recalled units in metrics

authoritative_sources.title: Recall Handbook
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The CPSC Recall Handbook is the official operating reference for retailer responsibilities, remedy communications, and corrective-action posture for unsafe consumer products.
used_for: boundary against safety or disclosure signoff

authoritative_sources.title: Fighting Identity Theft with the Red Flags Rule: A How-To Guide for Business
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC business guidance is the authoritative public baseline for recognizing identity-theft red flags and for determining when account, tender, or suspicious-return patterns must route to fraud or loss-prevention owners.

authoritative_sources.title: GS1 General Specifications Standard
authoritative_sources.source_class: Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GS1 is the recognized global standards body for retail identifiers and barcode usage. Its General Specifications are authoritative for GTIN, UPC, barcode, and product-identity linkage across retail systems.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: product identity and cross-system record mapping

authoritative_sources.title: Management's Discussion and Analysis, Selected Financial Data, and Supplementary Financial Information
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SEC KPI guidance is the authoritative public baseline for how operational metrics must be defined, contextualized, caveated, and updated when they are material to executive or investor-style disclosure.

workflow_stages.name: request framing and audience grounding
workflow_stages.description: Confirm the requested audience, reporting cadence, channel scope, product family, and tenant source-precedence rules before any metric work starts.
source_classes: Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance
Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: metric definition and denominator selection
workflow_stages.description: Define the metric, denominator, exclusions, inclusion rules, and source hierarchy for attachment, ASP, sell-through, refunds, service aging, and other electronics-retail measures.
source_classes: Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance
Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior

workflow_stages.name: record collection and cross-system validation
workflow_stages.description: Reconcile POS, OMS, CRM, service desk, inventory, pricing, and loss-prevention records to determine whether the requested metric can be supported confidently.
source_classes: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior

workflow_stages.name: analysis assembly and caveat design
workflow_stages.description: Assemble the analytical brief with explicit metric logic, refresh assumptions, thresholds, and caveats that preserve electronics-retail workflow context.
source_classes: Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance
Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: stronger-claim and disclosure review
workflow_stages.description: Stop descriptive analysis from turning into unsupported causal, policy, refund, pricing, safety, or board-ready disclosure claims without explicit evidence and approvals.
source_classes: Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance
Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance

workflow_stages.name: exception escalation and owner routing
workflow_stages.description: Route refund exceptions, pricing decisions, fraud-sensitive patterns, recall exposure, and other customer-impact cases to the correct adjacent specialist with complete evidence packaging.
source_classes: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance

artifacts: POS transactions, order records, BOPIS reservations, delivery statuses, and payment or refund records
promotion calendars, price tables, markdown records, coupon, bundle, and advertised-price artifacts
inventory snapshots, reservation states, open-box and floor-model status, and serial or IMEI-linked product records
service tickets, diagnosis-fee records, estimate or authorization artifacts, warranty claims, and service recovery notes
CRM notes, customer-impact records, appeasement requests, and guest-service escalation packages
loss-prevention cases, tender exception notes, chargeback indicators, suspicious-return records, and recall or stop-sale directives
metric dictionaries, retailer calendars, executive scorecards, disclosure templates, and approval matrices

allowed: Classify analytics requests and verify the minimum prerequisite records, definitions, and source hierarchy before recommendations.
Produce evidence-linked metric definitions, analytical briefs, reconciliation notes, and caveat packages without final commercial or safety judgment.
Escalate to adjacent authority when refund exceptions, pricing authority, fraud, safety, or conflicting policies determine the answer.
Refuse unsupported trend, benchmark, causal, or disclosure claims when data quality or denominator logic is insufficient.
escalate: Missing order, payment, refund, service, or inventory records materially change the interpretation.
Conflicts between POS, OMS, pricing, inventory, finance, or service records create unreconciled metric outcomes.
Customer harm, recall, refund exception, pricing authority, or fraud sensitivity changes whether the requested output is safe.
Tenant policy, jurisdictional rule, or approval-chain differences alter the metric or disclosure posture.
refuse: Any pricing, refund, warranty, fraud, safety, or disclosure signoff authority request.
Requests to publish certainty when source quality, source freshness, or definitions remain unresolved.
Final causal or policy claims where evidence is stale, conflicting, or unsupported by traceable records.

domain_failure_modes: Using a stale inventory extract and counting reserved BOPIS units as sellable availability without caveats.
Mixing open-box, floor-model, recalled, and new-in-box units into one sell-through or margin metric without explicit inclusion rules.
Treating POS and OMS refund differences as immaterial noise and publishing a single leakage figure anyway.
Comparing promotional periods across mismatched retailer calendars or undefined comparable-week logic.
Treating service-fee, diagnosis-fee, or warranty-remedy artifacts as interchangeable and overstating service performance or customer-remedy posture.
Turning correlation between bundles or promos and conversion into a causal claim without controlled evidence.
Absorbing suspicious serial mismatches, tender anomalies, or recall indicators into ordinary returns analytics instead of escalating.
Recommending refund amounts, markdown depth, or appeasement outcomes directly from analytics outputs without authority.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Refund or policy-exception request outside lane authority
Customer-impact issue with safety or fraud implications
Metric definition is ambiguous or disputed
Source data is incomplete, stale, or conflicting
Stakeholder asks for a claim or disclosure not supported by the data
Different systems disagree on the metric outcome
A stakeholder asks for stronger conclusions than the data supports

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: What booking, order, payment, or guest records govern the lane?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: The lane is governed by POS transactions, OMS orders, BOPIS reservations, delivery and service scheduling records, CRM notes, refund and payment records, service tickets, and related customer-impact artifacts. These records define what can be counted and what must remain caveated.

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: What refund, pricing, or policy constraints limit the workflow?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: FTC order, warranty, and pricing-claim guidance, NIST price-verification standards, official repair-operating guidance, and tenant refund, markdown, appeasement, and publication rules constrain how the lane can define and describe metrics. The lane may summarize these constraints but not override them.

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: Where does the lane stop and pricing, safety, or exception authority begin?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: The lane stops at evidence-backed descriptive analysis, source hierarchy, caveat design, and escalation packaging. Pricing decisions, refund exceptions, guest appeasements, fraud adjudication, recall or safety dispositions, and final disclosures belong to adjacent specialists or human approvers.
